title: Using Document Similarity Methods to create Parallel Datasets for Code Translation

structured.summary: The paper proposes generating noisy parallel code translation datasets by leveraging document similarity metrics, particularly Word Mover’s Distance, to automatically match code solving the same problems across languages. It demonstrates that pretrained code models fine-tuned on these noisy datasets maintain robust translation performance and expands supervised code translation to 10 programming languages beyond commonly studied pairs.

strengths.point: Addresses scarcity of parallel code datasets by automatic, unsupervised matching
strengths.evidence: The method generates noisy parallel corpora from monolingual corpora using document similarity without manual curation.

strengths.point: Empirical evaluation shows Word Mover’s Distance effectively identifies semantically similar code samples
strengths.evidence: WMD achieves higher match accuracy than TF-IDF, BM25, LDA, and LSI on both ground-truth and large-scale CodeNet datasets.

strengths.point: Demonstrates pretrained code models' robustness to noise in training data
strengths.evidence: Models fine-tuned with noisy parallel pairs show only mild performance degradation compared to ground-truth data, tolerating up to ~30% noise.

strengths.point: Practical contribution by creating and evaluating noisy parallel datasets across 10 diverse programming languages
strengths.evidence: Fine-tuned PLBART models trained on WMD-generated pairs outperform random baselines, including on less studied language pairs.

weaknesses.point: Lacks comparison to existing unsupervised or weakly supervised code translation methods
weaknesses.evidence: Related work and experimental baselines omit recent state-of-the-art approaches leveraging AST alignments, back-translation, or self-supervision.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient methodological details and analysis on key components
weaknesses.evidence: No rationale or ablation study for similarity threshold choice, embedding source and quality for WMD, or tokenization and preprocessing schemes.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics do not fully guarantee semantic or functional equivalence
weaknesses.evidence: Reliance on BLEU, CodeBLEU, Exact Match, and unit tests lacks human or formal semantic validation; unit test coverage adequacy is unaddressed.

weaknesses.point: One-to-one matching assumption without coverage analysis may limit dataset diversity
weaknesses.evidence: No analysis on unmatched documents or potential benefits of many-to-many pairings.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of scalability, computational efficiency, or practical deployment considerations
weaknesses.evidence: Pairwise similarity computations over large corpora may be costly; no mention of indexing or approximate methods.

weaknesses.point: No qualitative analysis of failure cases or error modes
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks examples or categorization of mismatches or poor translations.

weaknesses.point: Limited generalizability given exclusive use of CodeNet and educational problem datasets
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation on industrial or more heterogeneous codebases.

questions.question: How does the proposed noisy parallel data approach compare quantitatively and qualitatively to recent unsupervised/self-supervised code translation methods?
questions.motivation: Understanding relative gains over existing approaches is critical for assessing novelty and practical impact.

questions.question: What is the sensitivity of translation performance to the similarity threshold and embedding choices in the WMD metric?
questions.motivation: These parameters directly affect dataset quality and reproducibility; clarifying their impact aids method robustness.

questions.question: Can the method be extended to leverage structural code features (e.g., ASTs) in similarity for improved matching precision?
questions.motivation: Incorporating code semantics may reduce noise and improve translation quality.

questions.question: How well does the approach scale computationally to very large codebases, and are approximate matching methods feasible?
questions.motivation: Scalability is key for real-world adoption in industry-scale repositories.

critique_points.point_001: Lacks comparison to existing unsupervised or weakly supervised code translation methods. Related work and experimental baselines omit recent state-of-the-art approaches leveraging AST alignments, back-translation, or self-supervision
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient methodological details and analysis on key components. No rationale or ablation study for similarity threshold choice, embedding source and quality for WMD, or tokenization and preprocessing schemes
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation metrics do not fully guarantee semantic or functional equivalence. Reliance on BLEU, CodeBLEU, Exact Match, and unit tests lacks human or formal semantic validation; unit test coverage adequacy is unaddressed
critique_points.point_004: One-to-one matching assumption without coverage analysis may limit dataset diversity. No analysis on unmatched documents or potential benefits of many-to-many pairings
critique_points.point_005: No discussion of scalability, computational efficiency, or practical deployment considerations. Pairwise similarity computations over large corpora may be costly; no mention of indexing or approximate methods
critique_points.point_006: No qualitative analysis of failure cases or error modes. The paper lacks examples or categorization of mismatches or poor translations
critique_points.point_007: Limited generalizability given exclusive use of CodeNet and educational problem datasets. No evaluation on industrial or more heterogeneous codebases
